<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉偽装問題]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11562929]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Onsen Gisō Mondai''' (温泉偽装問題) refers to the collective issues regarding the actual practices of hot spring use that came to light in 2004. The main problems can be broadly categorized into the use of bath additives, the use of tap water, the ways in which spring sources were utilized, and unauthorized spring development.
==Overview== [[File:Sirahone spa kyoudou-noten-buro 2008.jpg|thumb|260px|right|The controversy began with the 2004 incident of colored bath additives.]] The controversy began with the bath additive coloring incident at Shirahone Onsen in Azumi Village, Minamiazumi District, Nagano Prefecture. Originally known for its milky-white springs, some facilities began to show reduced opacity around 1996. To maintain the traditional image of an opaque white bath, operators started adding coloring additives. Although the use of such additives did not violate the Onsen Law at the time, the lack of disclosure to customers and the popularity of the resort led to widespread media coverage. The Nagano Prefectural Government also released its own survey on onsen usage practices within the prefecture<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=県、調査結果を公表 入浴剤問題|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-07-28|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://mytown.asahi.com/nagano/news02.asp?c=33&kiji=176|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040819090324/http://mytown.asahi.com/nagano/news02.asp?c=33&kiji=176|archivedate=2004-08-19}}</ref>. As a result, reservations and day-trip visits in July dropped by 15% compared to 2003<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=7月のキャンセル3830人 白骨温泉の入浴剤問題|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-06|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0806/024.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040811035735/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0806/024.html|archivedate=2004-08-11}}</ref>.
In response, the Ministry of the Environment launched a nationwide survey of approximately 22,000 licensed inns, hotels, and public bathhouses to determine the presence of bath additive use and other practices such as water dilution, heating, and recirculation systems<ref name="asahi20040805">{{Cite news|和書|title=入浴剤使用、環境省が全国調査へ 2万2千カ所の温泉で|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-05|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0805/015.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040809233125/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0805/015.html|archivedate=2004-08-09}}</ref><ref name="tokyo20040805">{{Cite news|和書|title=入浴剤問題で全温泉調査へ 環境省|newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun|publisher=Chunichi Shimbun Tokyo Head Office|date=2004-08-05|accessdate=2025-03-06|url=http://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/00/sya/20040805/eve_____sya_____001.shtml|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040816211248/http://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/00/sya/20040805/eve_____sya_____001.shtml|archivedate=2004-08-16}}</ref>.
In August 2004, some ryokan in Ikaho Onsen, Gunma Prefecture, were found to be heating tap water in baths and labeling it as onsen water<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=水道水沸かした湯を「温泉」表示 群馬・伊香保温泉|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-09|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0809/019.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040811062852/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0809/019.html|archivedate=2004-08-11}}</ref>.
==Classification== * '''Bath additive use''' ** Facilities colored water to match traditional hues without disclosure; pre-disclosed medicinal baths were exempt. * '''Permanent tap water use''' ** Systems using boiled tap water labeled as onsen, sometimes including hot spring tax collection, often due to rights issues, high costs, or depleted sources. * '''Temporary tap water use''' ** Occurred when pumping equipment failed; failure to inform guests was the issue. * '''Excessive dilution''' ** Cases where spring water constituted less than 10% of bath volume, akin to tap water use. * '''Unauthorized spring development''' ** Drilling or sourcing without required permits; also included reverse misrepresentation where onsen was hidden.
==Aftermath== [[File:Shimobe-onsen.jpg|thumb|260px|right|At Shimobe Onsen, some springs were found not to meet legal criteria, though a new high-temperature source was developed in 2006 and named "Shimobe Oku no Yu Kōon Gensen".]] Subsequent reviews led to reanalysis of springs and onsen laws. Some members of the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association withdrew when their sources failed reanalysis<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=抜け穴だらけ“ぬるま湯”許さぬ…温泉法改正へ専門委|newspaper=Yomiuri Shimbun|publisher=Yomiuri Shimbun Company|date=2004-09-23|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/news/20040923i102.htm|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040923101227/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/news/20040923i102.htm|archivedate=2004-09-23}}</ref>. In 2005, the Onsen Law was amended to require posting of usage details—circulation, dilution, heating, additive use, and disinfectant use—on-site.
Prefectures and municipalities also established local certification systems, such as Nagano's "Safe, Honest Springs" program.

Although the use of such additives did not violate the Onsen Law at the time, the lack of disclosure to customers and the popularity of the resort led to widespread media coverage. The Nagano Prefectural Government also released its own survey on onsen usage practices within the prefecture<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=県、調査結果を公表 入浴剤問題|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-07-28|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://mytown.asahi.com/nagano/news02.asp?c=33&kiji=176|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040819090324/http://mytown.asahi.com/nagano/news02.asp?c=33&kiji=176|archivedate=2004-08-19}}</ref>. As a result, reservations and day-trip visits in July dropped by 15% compared to 2003<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=7月のキャンセル3830人 白骨温泉の入浴剤問題|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-06|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0806/024.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040811035735/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0806/024.html|archivedate=2004-08-11}}</ref>.
In response, the Ministry of the Environment launched a nationwide survey of approximately 22,000 licensed inns, hotels, and public bathhouses to determine the presence of bath additive use and other practices such as water dilution, heating, and recirculation systems<ref name="asahi20040805">{{Cite news|和書|title=入浴剤使用、環境省が全国調査へ 2万2千カ所の温泉で|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-05|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0805/015.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040809233125/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0805/015.html|archivedate=2004-08-09}}</ref><ref name="tokyo20040805">{{Cite news|和書|title=入浴剤問題で全温泉調査へ 環境省|newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun|publisher=Chunichi Shimbun Tokyo Head Office|date=2004-08-05|accessdate=2025-03-06|url=http://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/00/sya/20040805/eve_____sya_____001.shtml|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040816211248/http://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/00/sya/20040805/eve_____sya_____001.shtml|archivedate=2004-08-16}}</ref>.
In August 2004, some ryokan in Ikaho Onsen, Gunma Prefecture, were found to be heating tap water in baths and labeling it as onsen water<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=水道水沸かした湯を「温泉」表示 群馬・伊香保温泉|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-09|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0809/019.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040811062852/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0809/019.html|archivedate=2004-08-11}}</ref>.
==Classification== * '''Bath additive use''' ** Facilities colored water to match traditional hues without disclosure; pre-disclosed medicinal baths were exempt. * '''Permanent tap water use''' ** Systems using boiled tap water labeled as onsen, sometimes including hot spring tax collection, often due to rights issues, high costs, or depleted sources. * '''Temporary tap water use''' ** Occurred when pumping equipment failed; failure to inform guests was the issue. * '''Excessive dilution''' ** Cases where spring water constituted less than 10% of bath volume, akin to tap water use. * '''Unauthorized spring development''' ** Drilling or sourcing without required permits; also included reverse misrepresentation where onsen was hidden.
==Aftermath== [[File:Shimobe-onsen.jpg|thumb|260px|right|At Shimobe Onsen, some springs were found not to meet legal criteria, though a new high-temperature source was developed in 2006 and named "Shimobe Oku no Yu Kōon Gensen".]] Subsequent reviews led to reanalysis of springs and onsen laws. Some members of the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association withdrew when their sources failed reanalysis<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=抜け穴だらけ“ぬるま湯”許さぬ…温泉法改正へ専門委|newspaper=Yomiuri Shimbun|publisher=Yomiuri Shimbun Company|date=2004-09-23|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/news/20040923i102.htm|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040923101227/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/news/20040923i102.htm|archivedate=2004-09-23}}</ref>. In 2005, the Onsen Law was amended to require posting of usage details—circulation, dilution, heating, additive use, and disinfectant use—on-site.
Prefectures and municipalities also established local certification systems, such as Nagano's "Safe, Honest Springs" program.

Some members of the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association withdrew when their sources failed reanalysis<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=抜け穴だらけ“ぬるま湯”許さぬ…温泉法改正へ専門委|newspaper=Yomiuri Shimbun|publisher=Yomiuri Shimbun Company|date=2004-09-23|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/news/20040923i102.htm|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040923101227/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/news/20040923i102.htm|archivedate=2004-09-23}}</ref>. Although the use of such additives did not violate the Onsen Law at the time, the lack of disclosure to customers and the popularity of the resort led to widespread media coverage. The Nagano Prefectural Government also released its own survey on onsen usage practices within the prefecture<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=県、調査結果を公表 入浴剤問題|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-07-28|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://mytown.asahi.com/nagano/news02.asp?c=33&kiji=176|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040819090324/http://mytown.asahi.com/nagano/news02.asp?c=33&kiji=176|archivedate=2004-08-19}}</ref>. As a result, reservations and day-trip visits in July dropped by 15% compared to 2003<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=7月のキャンセル3830人 白骨温泉の入浴剤問題|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-06|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0806/024.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040811035735/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0806/024.html|archivedate=2004-08-11}}</ref>.
In response, the Ministry of the Environment launched a nationwide survey of approximately 22,000 licensed inns, hotels, and public bathhouses to determine the presence of bath additive use and other practices such as water dilution, heating, and recirculation systems<ref name="asahi20040805">{{Cite news|和書|title=入浴剤使用、環境省が全国調査へ 2万2千カ所の温泉で|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-05|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0805/015.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040809233125/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0805/015.html|archivedate=2004-08-09}}</ref><ref name="tokyo20040805">{{Cite news|和書|title=入浴剤問題で全温泉調査へ 環境省|newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun|publisher=Chunichi Shimbun Tokyo Head Office|date=2004-08-05|accessdate=2025-03-06|url=http://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/00/sya/20040805/eve_____sya_____001.shtml|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040816211248/http://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/00/sya/20040805/eve_____sya_____001.shtml|archivedate=2004-08-16}}</ref>.
In August 2004, some ryokan in Ikaho Onsen, Gunma Prefecture, were found to be heating tap water in baths and labeling it as onsen water<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=水道水沸かした湯を「温泉」表示 群馬・伊香保温泉|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-09|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0809/019.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040811062852/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0809/019.html|archivedate=2004-08-11}}</ref>.
==Classification== * '''Bath additive use''' ** Facilities colored water to match traditional hues without disclosure; pre-disclosed medicinal baths were exempt. * '''Permanent tap water use''' ** Systems using boiled tap water labeled as onsen, sometimes including hot spring tax collection, often due to rights issues, high costs, or depleted sources. * '''Temporary tap water use''' ** Occurred when pumping equipment failed; failure to inform guests was the issue. * '''Excessive dilution''' ** Cases where spring water constituted less than 10% of bath volume, akin to tap water use. * '''Unauthorized spring development''' ** Drilling or sourcing without required permits; also included reverse misrepresentation where onsen was hidden.
==Aftermath== [[File:Shimobe-onsen.jpg|thumb|260px|right|At Shimobe Onsen, some springs were found not to meet legal criteria, though a new high-temperature source was developed in 2006 and named "Shimobe Oku no Yu Kōon Gensen".]] Subsequent reviews led to reanalysis of springs and onsen laws. Some members of the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association withdrew when their sources failed reanalysis<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=抜け穴だらけ“ぬるま湯”許さぬ…温泉法改正へ専門委|newspaper=Yomiuri Shimbun|publisher=Yomiuri Shimbun Company|date=2004-09-23|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/news/20040923i102.htm|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040923101227/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/news/20040923i102.htm|archivedate=2004-09-23}}</ref>. In 2005, the Onsen Law was amended to require posting of usage details—circulation, dilution, heating, additive use, and disinfectant use—on-site.
Prefectures and municipalities also established local certification systems, such as Nagano's "Safe, Honest Springs" program.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉偽装問題]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11562929]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Onsen Gisō Mondai''' (温泉偽装問題) refers to the collective issues regarding the actual practices of hot spring use that came to light in 2004. The main problems can be broadly categorized into the use of bath additives, the use of tap water, the ways in which spring sources were utilized, and unauthorized spring development.
==Overview== [[File:Sirahone spa kyoudou-noten-buro 2008.jpg|thumb|260px|right|The controversy began with the 2004 incident of colored bath additives.]] The controversy began with the bath additive coloring incident at Shirahone Onsen in Azumi Village, Minamiazumi District, Nagano Prefecture. Originally known for its milky-white springs, some facilities began to show reduced opacity around 1996. To maintain the traditional image of an opaque white bath, operators started adding coloring additives. Although the use of such additives did not violate the Onsen Law at the time, the lack of disclosure to customers and the popularity of the resort led to widespread media coverage. The Nagano Prefectural Government also released its own survey on onsen usage practices within the prefecture<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=県、調査結果を公表 入浴剤問題|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-07-28|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://mytown.asahi.com/nagano/news02.asp?c=33&kiji=176|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040819090324/http://mytown.asahi.com/nagano/news02.asp?c=33&kiji=176|archivedate=2004-08-19}}</ref>. As a result, reservations and day-trip visits in July dropped by 15% compared to 2003<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=7月のキャンセル3830人 白骨温泉の入浴剤問題|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-06|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0806/024.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040811035735/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0806/024.html|archivedate=2004-08-11}}</ref>.
In response, the Ministry of the Environment launched a nationwide survey of approximately 22,000 licensed inns, hotels, and public bathhouses to determine the presence of bath additive use and other practices such as water dilution, heating, and recirculation systems<ref name="asahi20040805">{{Cite news|和書|title=入浴剤使用、環境省が全国調査へ 2万2千カ所の温泉で|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-05|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0805/015.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040809233125/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0805/015.html|archivedate=2004-08-09}}</ref><ref name="tokyo20040805">{{Cite news|和書|title=入浴剤問題で全温泉調査へ 環境省|newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun|publisher=Chunichi Shimbun Tokyo Head Office|date=2004-08-05|accessdate=2025-03-06|url=http://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/00/sya/20040805/eve_____sya_____001.shtml|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040816211248/http://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/00/sya/20040805/eve_____sya_____001.shtml|archivedate=2004-08-16}}</ref>.
In August 2004, some ryokan in Ikaho Onsen, Gunma Prefecture, were found to be heating tap water in baths and labeling it as onsen water<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=水道水沸かした湯を「温泉」表示 群馬・伊香保温泉|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-09|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0809/019.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040811062852/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0809/019.html|archivedate=2004-08-11}}</ref>.
==Classification== * '''Bath additive use''' ** Facilities colored water to match traditional hues without disclosure; pre-disclosed medicinal baths were exempt. * '''Permanent tap water use''' ** Systems using boiled tap water labeled as onsen, sometimes including hot spring tax collection, often due to rights issues, high costs, or depleted sources. * '''Temporary tap water use''' ** Occurred when pumping equipment failed; failure to inform guests was the issue. * '''Excessive dilution''' ** Cases where spring water constituted less than 10% of bath volume, akin to tap water use. * '''Unauthorized spring development''' ** Drilling or sourcing without required permits; also included reverse misrepresentation where onsen was hidden.
==Aftermath== [[File:Shimobe-onsen.jpg|thumb|260px|right|At Shimobe Onsen, some springs were found not to meet legal criteria, though a new high-temperature source was developed in 2006 and named "Shimobe Oku no Yu Kōon Gensen".]] Subsequent reviews led to reanalysis of springs and onsen laws. Some members of the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association withdrew when their sources failed reanalysis<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=抜け穴だらけ“ぬるま湯”許さぬ…温泉法改正へ専門委|newspaper=Yomiuri Shimbun|publisher=Yomiuri Shimbun Company|date=2004-09-23|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/news/20040923i102.htm|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040923101227/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/news/20040923i102.htm|archivedate=2004-09-23}}</ref>. In 2005, the Onsen Law was amended to require posting of usage details—circulation, dilution, heating, additive use, and disinfectant use—on-site.
Prefectures and municipalities also established local certification systems, such as Nagano's "Safe, Honest Springs" program.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉偽装問題]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11562929]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Onsen Gisō Mondai''' (温泉偽装問題) refers to the collective issues regarding the actual practices of hot spring use that came to light in 2004. The main problems can be broadly categorized into the use of bath additives, the use of tap water, the ways in which spring sources were utilized, and unauthorized spring development.
==Overview== [[File:Sirahone spa kyoudou-noten-buro 2008.jpg|thumb|260px|right|The controversy began with the 2004 incident of colored bath additives.]] The controversy began with the bath additive coloring incident at Shirahone Onsen in Azumi Village, Minamiazumi District, Nagano Prefecture. Originally known for its milky-white springs, some facilities began to show reduced opacity around 1996. To maintain the traditional image of an opaque white bath, operators started adding coloring additives. Although the use of such additives did not violate the Onsen Law at the time, the lack of disclosure to customers and the popularity of the resort led to widespread media coverage. The Nagano Prefectural Government also released its own survey on onsen usage practices within the prefecture<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=県、調査結果を公表 入浴剤問題|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-07-28|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://mytown.asahi.com/nagano/news02.asp?c=33&kiji=176|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040819090324/http://mytown.asahi.com/nagano/news02.asp?c=33&kiji=176|archivedate=2004-08-19}}</ref>. As a result, reservations and day-trip visits in July dropped by 15% compared to 2003<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=7月のキャンセル3830人 白骨温泉の入浴剤問題|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-06|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0806/024.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040811035735/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0806/024.html|archivedate=2004-08-11}}</ref>.
In response, the Ministry of the Environment launched a nationwide survey of approximately 22,000 licensed inns, hotels, and public bathhouses to determine the presence of bath additive use and other practices such as water dilution, heating, and recirculation systems<ref name="asahi20040805">{{Cite news|和書|title=入浴剤使用、環境省が全国調査へ 2万2千カ所の温泉で|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-05|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0805/015.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040809233125/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0805/015.html|archivedate=2004-08-09}}</ref><ref name="tokyo20040805">{{Cite news|和書|title=入浴剤問題で全温泉調査へ 環境省|newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun|publisher=Chunichi Shimbun Tokyo Head Office|date=2004-08-05|accessdate=2025-03-06|url=http://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/00/sya/20040805/eve_____sya_____001.shtml|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040816211248/http://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/00/sya/20040805/eve_____sya_____001.shtml|archivedate=2004-08-16}}</ref>.
In August 2004, some ryokan in Ikaho Onsen, Gunma Prefecture, were found to be heating tap water in baths and labeling it as onsen water<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=水道水沸かした湯を「温泉」表示 群馬・伊香保温泉|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-09|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0809/019.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040811062852/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0809/019.html|archivedate=2004-08-11}}</ref>.
==Classification== * '''Bath additive use''' ** Facilities colored water to match traditional hues without disclosure; pre-disclosed medicinal baths were exempt. * '''Permanent tap water use''' ** Systems using boiled tap water labeled as onsen, sometimes including hot spring tax collection, often due to rights issues, high costs, or depleted sources. * '''Temporary tap water use''' ** Occurred when pumping equipment failed; failure to inform guests was the issue. * '''Excessive dilution''' ** Cases where spring water constituted less than 10% of bath volume, akin to tap water use. * '''Unauthorized spring development''' ** Drilling or sourcing without required permits; also included reverse misrepresentation where onsen was hidden.
==Aftermath== [[File:Shimobe-onsen.jpg|thumb|260px|right|At Shimobe Onsen, some springs were found not to meet legal criteria, though a new high-temperature source was developed in 2006 and named "Shimobe Oku no Yu Kōon Gensen".]] Subsequent reviews led to reanalysis of springs and onsen laws. Some members of the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association withdrew when their sources failed reanalysis<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=抜け穴だらけ“ぬるま湯”許さぬ…温泉法改正へ専門委|newspaper=Yomiuri Shimbun|publisher=Yomiuri Shimbun Company|date=2004-09-23|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/news/20040923i102.htm|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040923101227/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/news/20040923i102.htm|archivedate=2004-09-23}}</ref>. In 2005, the Onsen Law was amended to require posting of usage details—circulation, dilution, heating, additive use, and disinfectant use—on-site.
Prefectures and municipalities also established local certification systems, such as Nagano's "Safe, Honest Springs" program.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉偽装問題]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉偽装問題]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉偽装問題]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11562929]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Onsen Gisō Mondai''' (温泉偽装問題) refers to the collective issues regarding the actual practices of hot spring use that came to light in 2004. The main problems can be broadly categorized into the use of bath additives, the use of tap water, the ways in which spring sources were utilized, and unauthorized spring development.
==Overview== [[File:Sirahone spa kyoudou-noten-buro 2008.jpg|thumb|260px|right|The controversy began with the 2004 incident of colored bath additives.]] The controversy began with the bath additive coloring incident at Shirahone Onsen in Azumi Village, Minamiazumi District, Nagano Prefecture. Originally known for its milky-white springs, some facilities began to show reduced opacity around 1996. To maintain the traditional image of an opaque white bath, operators started adding coloring additives. Although the use of such additives did not violate the Onsen Law at the time, the lack of disclosure to customers and the popularity of the resort led to widespread media coverage. The Nagano Prefectural Government also released its own survey on onsen usage practices within the prefecture<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=県、調査結果を公表 入浴剤問題|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-07-28|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://mytown.asahi.com/nagano/news02.asp?c=33&kiji=176|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040819090324/http://mytown.asahi.com/nagano/news02.asp?c=33&kiji=176|archivedate=2004-08-19}}</ref>. As a result, reservations and day-trip visits in July dropped by 15% compared to 2003<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=7月のキャンセル3830人 白骨温泉の入浴剤問題|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-06|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0806/024.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040811035735/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0806/024.html|archivedate=2004-08-11}}</ref>.
In response, the Ministry of the Environment launched a nationwide survey of approximately 22,000 licensed inns, hotels, and public bathhouses to determine the presence of bath additive use and other practices such as water dilution, heating, and recirculation systems<ref name="asahi20040805">{{Cite news|和書|title=入浴剤使用、環境省が全国調査へ 2万2千カ所の温泉で|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-05|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0805/015.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040809233125/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0805/015.html|archivedate=2004-08-09}}</ref><ref name="tokyo20040805">{{Cite news|和書|title=入浴剤問題で全温泉調査へ 環境省|newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun|publisher=Chunichi Shimbun Tokyo Head Office|date=2004-08-05|accessdate=2025-03-06|url=http://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/00/sya/20040805/eve_____sya_____001.shtml|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040816211248/http://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/00/sya/20040805/eve_____sya_____001.shtml|archivedate=2004-08-16}}</ref>.
In August 2004, some ryokan in Ikaho Onsen, Gunma Prefecture, were found to be heating tap water in baths and labeling it as onsen water<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=水道水沸かした湯を「温泉」表示 群馬・伊香保温泉|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-09|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0809/019.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040811062852/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0809/019.html|archivedate=2004-08-11}}</ref>.
==Classification== * '''Bath additive use''' ** Facilities colored water to match traditional hues without disclosure; pre-disclosed medicinal baths were exempt. * '''Permanent tap water use''' ** Systems using boiled tap water labeled as onsen, sometimes including hot spring tax collection, often due to rights issues, high costs, or depleted sources. * '''Temporary tap water use''' ** Occurred when pumping equipment failed; failure to inform guests was the issue. * '''Excessive dilution''' ** Cases where spring water constituted less than 10% of bath volume, akin to tap water use. * '''Unauthorized spring development''' ** Drilling or sourcing without required permits; also included reverse misrepresentation where onsen was hidden.
==Aftermath== [[File:Shimobe-onsen.jpg|thumb|260px|right|At Shimobe Onsen, some springs were found not to meet legal criteria, though a new high-temperature source was developed in 2006 and named "Shimobe Oku no Yu Kōon Gensen".]] Subsequent reviews led to reanalysis of springs and onsen laws. Some members of the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association withdrew when their sources failed reanalysis<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=抜け穴だらけ“ぬるま湯”許さぬ…温泉法改正へ専門委|newspaper=Yomiuri Shimbun|publisher=Yomiuri Shimbun Company|date=2004-09-23|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/news/20040923i102.htm|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040923101227/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/news/20040923i102.htm|archivedate=2004-09-23}}</ref>. In 2005, the Onsen Law was amended to require posting of usage details—circulation, dilution, heating, additive use, and disinfectant use—on-site.
Prefectures and municipalities also established local certification systems, such as Nagano's "Safe, Honest Springs" program.
==Notes== {{Reflist}}

'''Onsen Gisō Mondai''' (温泉偽装問題) refers to the collective issues regarding the actual practices of hot spring use that came to light in 2004. The main problems can be broadly categorized into the use of bath additives, the use of tap water, the ways in which spring sources were utilized, and unauthorized spring development.
==Overview== [[File:Sirahone spa kyoudou-noten-buro 2008.jpg|thumb|260px|right|The controversy began with the 2004 incident of colored bath additives.]] The controversy began with the bath additive coloring incident at Shirahone Onsen in Azumi Village, Minamiazumi District, Nagano Prefecture. Originally known for its milky-white springs, some facilities began to show reduced opacity around 1996. To maintain the traditional image of an opaque white bath, operators started adding coloring additives. Although the use of such additives did not violate the Onsen Law at the time, the lack of disclosure to customers and the popularity of the resort led to widespread media coverage. The Nagano Prefectural Government also released its own survey on onsen usage practices within the prefecture<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=県、調査結果を公表 入浴剤問題|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-07-28|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://mytown.asahi.com/nagano/news02.asp?c=33&kiji=176|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040819090324/http://mytown.asahi.com/nagano/news02.asp?c=33&kiji=176|archivedate=2004-08-19}}</ref>. As a result, reservations and day-trip visits in July dropped by 15% compared to 2003<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=7月のキャンセル3830人 白骨温泉の入浴剤問題|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-06|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0806/024.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040811035735/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0806/024.html|archivedate=2004-08-11}}</ref>.
In response, the Ministry of the Environment launched a nationwide survey of approximately 22,000 licensed inns, hotels, and public bathhouses to determine the presence of bath additive use and other practices such as water dilution, heating, and recirculation systems<ref name="asahi20040805">{{Cite news|和書|title=入浴剤使用、環境省が全国調査へ 2万2千カ所の温泉で|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-05|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0805/015.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040809233125/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0805/015.html|archivedate=2004-08-09}}</ref><ref name="tokyo20040805">{{Cite news|和書|title=入浴剤問題で全温泉調査へ 環境省|newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun|publisher=Chunichi Shimbun Tokyo Head Office|date=2004-08-05|accessdate=2025-03-06|url=http://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/00/sya/20040805/eve_____sya_____001.shtml|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040816211248/http://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/00/sya/20040805/eve_____sya_____001.shtml|archivedate=2004-08-16}}</ref>.
In August 2004, some ryokan in Ikaho Onsen, Gunma Prefecture, were found to be heating tap water in baths and labeling it as onsen water<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=水道水沸かした湯を「温泉」表示 群馬・伊香保温泉|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-09|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0809/019.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040811062852/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0809/019.html|archivedate=2004-08-11}}</ref>.
==Classification== * '''Bath additive use''' ** Facilities colored water to match traditional hues without disclosure; pre-disclosed medicinal baths were exempt. * '''Permanent tap water use''' ** Systems using boiled tap water labeled as onsen, sometimes including hot spring tax collection, often due to rights issues, high costs, or depleted sources. * '''Temporary tap water use''' ** Occurred when pumping equipment failed; failure to inform guests was the issue. * '''Excessive dilution''' ** Cases where spring water constituted less than 10% of bath volume, akin to tap water use. * '''Unauthorized spring development''' ** Drilling or sourcing without required permits; also included reverse misrepresentation where onsen was hidden.
==Aftermath== [[File:Shimobe-onsen.jpg|thumb|260px|right|At Shimobe Onsen, some springs were found not to meet legal criteria, though a new high-temperature source was developed in 2006 and named "Shimobe Oku no Yu Kōon Gensen".]] Subsequent reviews led to reanalysis of springs and onsen laws. Some members of the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association withdrew when their sources failed reanalysis<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=抜け穴だらけ“ぬるま湯”許さぬ…温泉法改正へ専門委|newspaper=Yomiuri Shimbun|publisher=Yomiuri Shimbun Company|date=2004-09-23|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/news/20040923i102.htm|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040923101227/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/news/20040923i102.htm|archivedate=2004-09-23}}</ref>. In 2005, the Onsen Law was amended to require posting of usage details—circulation, dilution, heating, additive use, and disinfectant use—on-site.
Prefectures and municipalities also established local certification systems, such as Nagano's "Safe, Honest Springs" program.
==Notes== {{Reflist}}

Although the use of such additives did not violate the Onsen Law at the time, the lack of disclosure to customers and the popularity of the resort led to widespread media coverage. The Nagano Prefectural Government also released its own survey on onsen usage practices within the prefecture<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=県、調査結果を公表 入浴剤問題|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-07-28|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://mytown.asahi.com/nagano/news02.asp?c=33&kiji=176|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040819090324/http://mytown.asahi.com/nagano/news02.asp?c=33&kiji=176|archivedate=2004-08-19}}</ref>. As a result, reservations and day-trip visits in July dropped by 15% compared to 2003<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=7月のキャンセル3830人 白骨温泉の入浴剤問題|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-06|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0806/024.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040811035735/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0806/024.html|archivedate=2004-08-11}}</ref>.
In response, the Ministry of the Environment launched a nationwide survey of approximately 22,000 licensed inns, hotels, and public bathhouses to determine the presence of bath additive use and other practices such as water dilution, heating, and recirculation systems<ref name="asahi20040805">{{Cite news|和書|title=入浴剤使用、環境省が全国調査へ 2万2千カ所の温泉で|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-05|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0805/015.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040809233125/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0805/015.html|archivedate=2004-08-09}}</ref><ref name="tokyo20040805">{{Cite news|和書|title=入浴剤問題で全温泉調査へ 環境省|newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun|publisher=Chunichi Shimbun Tokyo Head Office|date=2004-08-05|accessdate=2025-03-06|url=http://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/00/sya/20040805/eve_____sya_____001.shtml|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040816211248/http://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/00/sya/20040805/eve_____sya_____001.shtml|archivedate=2004-08-16}}</ref>.
In August 2004, some ryokan in Ikaho Onsen, Gunma Prefecture, were found to be heating tap water in baths and labeling it as onsen water<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=水道水沸かした湯を「温泉」表示 群馬・伊香保温泉|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-09|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0809/019.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040811062852/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0809/019.html|archivedate=2004-08-11}}</ref>.
==Classification== * '''Bath additive use''' ** Facilities colored water to match traditional hues without disclosure; pre-disclosed medicinal baths were exempt. * '''Permanent tap water use''' ** Systems using boiled tap water labeled as onsen, sometimes including hot spring tax collection, often due to rights issues, high costs, or depleted sources. * '''Temporary tap water use''' ** Occurred when pumping equipment failed; failure to inform guests was the issue. * '''Excessive dilution''' ** Cases where spring water constituted less than 10% of bath volume, akin to tap water use. * '''Unauthorized spring development''' ** Drilling or sourcing without required permits; also included reverse misrepresentation where onsen was hidden.
==Aftermath== [[File:Shimobe-onsen.jpg|thumb|260px|right|At Shimobe Onsen, some springs were found not to meet legal criteria, though a new high-temperature source was developed in 2006 and named "Shimobe Oku no Yu Kōon Gensen".]] Subsequent reviews led to reanalysis of springs and onsen laws. Some members of the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association withdrew when their sources failed reanalysis<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=抜け穴だらけ“ぬるま湯”許さぬ…温泉法改正へ専門委|newspaper=Yomiuri Shimbun|publisher=Yomiuri Shimbun Company|date=2004-09-23|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/news/20040923i102.htm|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040923101227/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/news/20040923i102.htm|archivedate=2004-09-23}}</ref>. In 2005, the Onsen Law was amended to require posting of usage details—circulation, dilution, heating, additive use, and disinfectant use—on-site.
Prefectures and municipalities also established local certification systems, such as Nagano's "Safe, Honest Springs" program.
==Notes== {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
'''Onsen Gisō Mondai''' (温泉偽装問題) refers to the collective issues regarding the actual practices of hot spring use that came to light in 2004. The main problems can be broadly categorized into the use of bath additives, the use of tap water, the ways in which spring sources were utilized, and unauthorized spring development.
==Overview== [[File:Sirahone spa kyoudou-noten-buro 2008.jpg|thumb|260px|right|The controversy began with the 2004 incident of colored bath additives.]] The controversy began with the bath additive coloring incident at Shirahone Onsen in Azumi Village, Minamiazumi District, Nagano Prefecture. Originally known for its milky-white springs, some facilities began to show reduced opacity around 1996. To maintain the traditional image of an opaque white bath, operators started adding coloring additives. Although the use of such additives did not violate the Onsen Law at the time, the lack of disclosure to customers and the popularity of the resort led to widespread media coverage. The Nagano Prefectural Government also released its own survey on onsen usage practices within the prefecture<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=県、調査結果を公表 入浴剤問題|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-07-28|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://mytown.asahi.com/nagano/news02.asp?c=33&kiji=176|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040819090324/http://mytown.asahi.com/nagano/news02.asp?c=33&kiji=176|archivedate=2004-08-19}}</ref>. As a result, reservations and day-trip visits in July dropped by 15% compared to 2003<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=7月のキャンセル3830人 白骨温泉の入浴剤問題|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-06|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0806/024.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040811035735/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0806/024.html|archivedate=2004-08-11}}</ref>.
In response, the Ministry of the Environment launched a nationwide survey of approximately 22,000 licensed inns, hotels, and public bathhouses to determine the presence of bath additive use and other practices such as water dilution, heating, and recirculation systems<ref name="asahi20040805">{{Cite news|和書|title=入浴剤使用、環境省が全国調査へ 2万2千カ所の温泉で|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-05|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0805/015.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040809233125/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0805/015.html|archivedate=2004-08-09}}</ref><ref name="tokyo20040805">{{Cite news|和書|title=入浴剤問題で全温泉調査へ 環境省|newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun|publisher=Chunichi Shimbun Tokyo Head Office|date=2004-08-05|accessdate=2025-03-06|url=http://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/00/sya/20040805/eve_____sya_____001.shtml|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040816211248/http://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/00/sya/20040805/eve_____sya_____001.shtml|archivedate=2004-08-16}}</ref>.
In August 2004, some ryokan in Ikaho Onsen, Gunma Prefecture, were found to be heating tap water in baths and labeling it as onsen water<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=水道水沸かした湯を「温泉」表示 群馬・伊香保温泉|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Company|date=2004-08-09|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0809/019.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040811062852/http://www.asahi.com/national/update/0809/019.html|archivedate=2004-08-11}}</ref>.
==Classification== * '''Bath additive use''' ** Facilities colored water to match traditional hues without disclosure; pre-disclosed medicinal baths were exempt. * '''Permanent tap water use''' ** Systems using boiled tap water labeled as onsen, sometimes including hot spring tax collection, often due to rights issues, high costs, or depleted sources. * '''Temporary tap water use''' ** Occurred when pumping equipment failed; failure to inform guests was the issue. * '''Excessive dilution''' ** Cases where spring water constituted less than 10% of bath volume, akin to tap water use. * '''Unauthorized spring development''' ** Drilling or sourcing without required permits; also included reverse misrepresentation where onsen was hidden.
==Aftermath== [[File:Shimobe-onsen.jpg|thumb|260px|right|At Shimobe Onsen, some springs were found not to meet legal criteria, though a new high-temperature source was developed in 2006 and named "Shimobe Oku no Yu Kōon Gensen".]] Subsequent reviews led to reanalysis of springs and onsen laws. Some members of the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association withdrew when their sources failed reanalysis<ref>{{Cite news|和書|title=抜け穴だらけ“ぬるま湯”許さぬ…温泉法改正へ専門委|newspaper=Yomiuri Shimbun|publisher=Yomiuri Shimbun Company|date=2004-09-23|accessdate=2024-11-06|url=http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/news/20040923i102.htm|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20040923101227/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/news/20040923i102.htm|archivedate=2004-09-23}}</ref>. In 2005, the Onsen Law was amended to require posting of usage details—circulation, dilution, heating, additive use, and disinfectant use—on-site.
Prefectures and municipalities also established local certification systems, such as Nagano's "Safe, Honest Springs" program.
